1: After Saul returns from pursuing the Philistines, he is told David is in the wilderness of En-gedi. The transition is rapid: the Philistine crisis resolved, Saul immediately resumes the pursuit. The obsession is undiverted. Three thousand chosen men of Israel are deployed specifically against one fugitive — a gross disproportion that the narrative leaves as silent indictment.
2: Saul takes three thousand chosen men from all Israel and goes to seek David and his men in front of the Rocks of the Wild Goats. The specific terrain — wild goat rocks — is the terrain of En-gedi's cliff faces and caves. The setting is also a theological image: the wild goat (yāʿēl) is the creature of inaccessible heights, living where humans cannot easily go. David has retreated into the wild goat's territory.
3: Saul comes to the sheep-folds along the way, where there was a cave, and goes in to relieve himself. David and his men are sitting in the innermost parts of the cave. The comedy of the scene is exquisite: the king seeking the fugitive enters the very cave where the fugitive is hidden, for the most mundane of purposes. The cave is deep enough that the darkness conceals David's band from Saul's sight. Providence has placed them together in the most intimate possible proximity.
4: David's men whisper: 'Here is the day of which YHWH said to you, I will give your enemy into your hand, and you shall do to him as it seems good to you.' The men interpret the moment as divine fulfillment — an oracle apparently given earlier that David's enemy would be delivered to him. Their theological interpretation is not wrong (YHWH has brought Saul into the cave), but their conclusion — that David should now kill Saul — is wrong. Providence can create the opportunity without mandating the action.
5: David rises and cuts off a corner of Saul's robe (kānāp) secretly. The corner of the robe has covenant significance: the kānāp was the fringed hem that bore the owner's identity-seal in the ANE — like a signature. Grabbing or cutting the corner of a ruler's garment was a gesture with legal and covenant implications. David is claiming something — perhaps the kingdom itself — but still cannot bring himself to harm the man.
6: Afterward, David's heart struck him because he had cut off the corner of Saul's robe. He says: 'YHWH forbid that I should do this thing to my lord, YHWH's anointed, to put out my hand against him, seeing he is YHWH's anointed (māšîaḥ YHWH).' David's conscience (wayyak lēḇ-dāwid ʾōṯô) — literally, 'his heart struck him' — is pricked even by the robe-cutting. If cutting a garment corner troubles him, how much more the taking of life. The inviolability of the anointed is not a political calculation for David but a theological conviction.
7: David restrains his men with words and does not allow them to attack Saul. Saul leaves the cave and goes on his way. The contrast between David's restraint (against his own men's urging) and Saul's earlier inability to restrain himself (11:13 aside) is the chapter's moral argument. David's authority over his men is the authority of covenant principle over military opportunity.
8: Afterward David rises and goes out of the cave and calls after Saul: 'My lord the king!' When Saul looks behind him, David bows with his face to the earth and pays homage. David's posture — bowing before the king he just had in his power — is the enacted argument of everything he will say. The physical deference demonstrates that the power relationship has not changed in David's mind; Saul is still the king and David is still the subject.
9: David addresses Saul: 'Why do you listen to people who say, David is seeking your harm? Behold, this day your eyes have seen how YHWH gave you today into my hand in the cave. Some said to kill you, but my eye had pity on you.' David frames the confrontation as an evidence-presentation: the robe corner he is about to produce is exhibit A in the argument that he had the opportunity to harm Saul and chose not to. He is also exposing Saul's advisors as the source of the paranoia.
10: 'I said I will not put out my hand against my lord, for he is YHWH's anointed.' The theological principle is given explicitly: the reason for restraint is not strategic (David doesn't want civil war), not psychological (David doesn't hate Saul yet), but covenantal — Saul is YHWH's anointed. As long as YHWH has designated Saul as king, David will not act against that designation by human force. The anointing creates an inviolability that supersedes David's own right of self-defense.
11: 'See, my father, see the corner of your robe in my hand. For in that I cut off the corner of your robe and did not kill you, you may know and see that there is no wrong or treason in my hands.' David produces the evidence — the robe corner — and makes the inference explicit: if I were your enemy I would have killed you; I cut only your robe; therefore I am not your enemy. The word 'father' (ʾāḇî) is David's term of address for Saul — the covenant relationship of son to father-in-law is still claimed even in this confrontation.
12: 'YHWH judge between me and you; YHWH avenge me against you, but my hand shall not be against you.' The appeal to divine judgment rather than human vindication is the legal principle that governs David's behavior throughout the fugitive years: YHWH is the judge between the anointed who has been wronged and the anointed who has wronged him. David removes himself from the role of prosecutor, judge, and executioner — all of which he could legally claim — and assigns all three to YHWH.
13: 'As the proverb of the ancients says, Out of the wicked comes wickedness. But my hand shall not be against you.' David cites a proverbial principle: evil actions flow from an evil character. The proverb is an implicit self-testimony: he has not acted wickedly, therefore (unlike Saul) he is not wicked. The appeal to proverbial wisdom grounds his self-defense in shared cultural understanding rather than merely personal claim.
14: 'After whom has the king of Israel come out? After whom are you pursuing? A dead dog! A single flea!' David uses the self-deprecation of ANE diplomatic speech — presenting himself as insignificant — to highlight the absurdity of a royal army pursuing one man through the wilderness. 'A dead dog' and 'a single flea' are hyperboles of insignificance that reframe the pursuit as beneath royal dignity.
15: 'YHWH will be judge and will decide between me and you. He will see, and he will plead my cause and deliver me from your hand.' The triple appeal — YHWH as judge, advocate (rîḇ), and deliverer — is a formal legal declaration. David is not merely expressing hope; he is filing a legal brief with the divine court. The rîḇ-vocabulary (lawsuit, to plead a cause) places the confrontation within the covenant lawsuit framework that Samuel had used in ch 12.
16: When David finishes, Saul says: 'Is this your voice, my son David?' And Saul weeps. The weeping is genuine — the narrative gives no indication of manipulation. Saul recognizes in David's restraint something he himself no longer possesses: covenant faithfulness to a rival. The address 'my son David' (bənî dāwîḏ) acknowledges the covenant relationship David has just claimed with his address of 'my father.'
17: Saul says: 'You are more righteous than I (ṣaddîq ʾattāh mimmennî), for you have repaid me good, whereas I have repaid you evil.' Saul's confession is the chapter's moral climax: the king who has been pursuing David with three thousand men acknowledges that David is in the right. The two terms ṣaddîq (righteous) and rāʿ (evil) are the covenant categories of those who keep faith and those who break it. Saul has just pronounced covenant judgment on himself.
18: 'You have declared this day how you have dealt well with me, in that you did not kill me when YHWH put me into your hands.' Saul's acknowledgment is precise: YHWH had given Saul into David's hand — the opportunity was genuine and divinely arranged — and David chose not to use it. The theology of restraint is confirmed by the king himself.
19: 'For if a man finds his enemy, will he let him go away safe? So may YHWH reward you with good for what you have done to me this day.' The rhetorical question identifies what David did as extraordinary: no one releases a captured enemy. The reward-prayer is Saul invoking YHWH's blessing on the man he has been trying to kill — a covenant contradiction Saul himself seems to sense.
20: 'And now, behold, I know that you shall surely be king, and that the kingdom of Israel shall be established in your hand.' Saul's acknowledgment of David's future kingship is the definitive royal concession. It confirms what Jonathan, Samuel, and Abigail have all said. The irony is that Saul's own recognition of YHWH's purpose does not change his behavior: he will resume the pursuit in ch 26. Knowledge of divine will and submission to it can be permanently severed.
21: 'Swear to me therefore by YHWH that you will not cut off my offspring after me, and that you will not destroy my name out of my father's house.' Saul's request reflects the ANE practice of new dynasties eliminating potential claimants from the previous dynasty. His request is essentially a dynastic-continuity oath — he is asking David to spare the family line even after taking the throne. The oath he asks for is entirely reasonable; ANE parallel examples abound.
22: David swears to Saul. Then Saul goes home and David and his men go up to the stronghold. The chapter ends in separation: the oath is sworn, the covenant gesture is complete, but the structural situation is unchanged. Saul returns to his palace; David returns to the wilderness. No political resolution has occurred, only a moral one. The pursuit will resume (ch 26), confirming that Saul's genuine moments of clarity cannot be sustained against the pressure of the evil spirit.
